summary: Gruber reviews the 2013 retina iPad Mini and finds it to be a far more significant upgrade than expected — not merely a year-behind version of the full-size iPad, but functionally identical to the iPad Air. He details how performance, display quality, battery life, and charging speed are all on par with the Air, defying his prediction that the Mini would trail by a generation. The review argues this eliminates the compromises that previously defined the Mini lineup, making the choice between Air and Mini purely about size preference. He notes this is effectively a two-year upgrade compressed into one year, and suggests the rebranding of the full-size iPad to 'iPad Air' reflects that neither model is the 'main' iPad anymore.
key_points: The retina iPad Mini matches the iPad Air in CPU performance, display quality, cameras, and battery life — eliminating the generation-behind gap that defined the original Mini
Battery and charging tests show effectively identical results between the Mini and Air, with the Mini actually charging faster despite shipping with a less powerful adapter
The upgrade from original Mini to retina Mini is so substantial it feels like a two-year leap rather than an annual refresh
The rebranding from 'iPad' to 'iPad Air' signals Apple no longer considers the 9.7-inch model the default — both sizes are now equal peers
Apple's pattern of shrinking the time each new form factor spends without a retina display suggests future devices may debut retina from day one
money_quotes: Last year's iPad 4 and Mini were two very different iPads. This year's new Air and Mini are simply two sizes of the same iPad.
As I tested and just plain used the new iPad Mini, I kept thinking there had to be a catch — some sort of way that the device is less powerful or useful than the iPad Air. There is no catch.
This is the iPad Mini I expected to see next year, in 2014. But here it is today, in my hand.
There is no drawback to the iPad Mini going retina.
Last year Apple billed the Mini as 'every inch an iPad', and that was true, but it was every inch an iPad 2. This year, it's every inch a top-of-the-line iPad.
There no longer is a main or regular or standard iPad.

Just one year ago, those were the compromises Apple was forced to make. They could shrink the year old iPad 2 into the Mini form factor, or go retina and A6X with a thicker and heavier battery. This year, there are no compromises, there is no or.
key_insight: The retina iPad Mini represents a rare moment where Apple eliminated all compromises in a single generation, turning what was a lesser product into a full peer of its larger sibling.
